nativeRange.habitat: Creeks, swamps, streams, and pools in humid forested highland savannah and areas of rainforest; some habitats may periodically dry out but often retain water year-round

diet.notes: Micropredator with a relatively large mouth for its size. Has been documented eating small live and frozen foods including daphnia, baby brine shrimp (Artemia), blackworms, bloodworm, and micro-worms. Frozen foods are generally accepted readily; micropellets can be offered occasionally but meaty foods are generally preferred. Males have been known to attempt eating very small nano fish, so tankmates should be of comparable size or larger.

avoidWith: other male Fundulopanchax gardneri (severe aggression between males)
other killifish species males in small tanks
nano fish smaller than ~2 cm (predation risk)

summary: Iridescent blue-green West African killifish with red-spotted flanks. Males are strikingly colored and highly aggressive toward each other; best kept as a 1M:2–3F trio. A hardy, non-annual species considered a gateway killifish for hobbyists.
careNotes:
Non-annual species: unlike the well-known Nothobranchius killifish, Fundulopanchax gardneri does not require a dry-season diapause and tends to live 2–5 years in well-maintained aquariums. Although some natural habitats may periodically dry out, the species generally has access to water year-round.

Male aggression: males tend to be highly aggressive toward conspecific males and will often fight to the point of injury or death in confined spaces. A single male kept with 2–3 females is the generally advised approach. If housing a group of males, a larger tank (100L+) with dense visual breaks (tall plants, driftwood, spawning mops) may allow a colony, but close monitoring is recommended.

Female ratio: stocking 2–3 females per male is generally recommended to distribute male attention and reduce stress on individual females.

Escape risk: this species is considered a confirmed jumper and has been known to exit aquariums through very small gaps. A tight-fitting lid is mandatory, not optional.

Geographic and ornamental variants: several nominal subspecies are documented — F. g. lacustris (Lake Ejagham population, Cameroon), F. g. mamfensis (Mamfe/Cameroon region), and F. g. nigerianus (Nigerian populations, including the deeper-blue 'Nigerian' strain popular in the hobby). An ornamental 'Mama Gold' strain (gold/yellow body coloration) and albino strains exist as captive-bred line variants. Hobbyists often refer to the 'Nigerian' form as having more intense blue coloration, while the 'Cameroon' form (especially mamfensis) tends toward gold or olive. These variants are husbandry-compatible — no special care differences have been documented.

Water hardness sources varied: SeriouslyFish reported a very broad range (18–179 ppm, roughly gH 1–10 dH); FishBase gave a narrower 5–8 dH. The composite gH range of 4–12 dH represents a reasonable average across sources. The species has been documented tolerating moderate hardness, unlike many softwater West African species.

Cooler temperature tolerance: this species is commonly kept at lower temperatures than strict tropical fish. Many keepers report good health at 20–23°C, and spawning can be triggered by slight temperature drops. 25–26°C is near the upper practical limit; sustained higher temperatures are generally not recommended.

Name etymology: 'killi' derives from the Dutch word for 'creek' or 'channel' — not 'killer'. The species is named after British zoologist E. A. Boulenger collected early specimens under the direction of W. A. Gardner. The common trade name 'Steel-Blue Killifish' refers to the male's iridescent blue-green metallic sheen.

Egg transport: dry-incubated eggs can survive 30–60 days in moist peat or spawning mop material and are commonly traded and shipped between hobbyists by mail, making this an accessible entry point into the killifish hobby.

adultSizeCm note: SeriouslyFish reports 50–55 mm standard length; FishBase reports 6.5 cm total length; Aquarium Co-Op top-5 article states 6 cm. The composite range (5.0–6.5 cm TL) spans these sources. Females tend to be somewhat smaller and less colorful than males.
breedingNotes: Commonly considered one of the more accessible killifish to breed — often described as a gateway species for killifish hobbyists. Eggs can be laid in spawning mops, fine-leaved plants, or directly on substrate (semi-annual strategy — not strictly annual). Two incubation methods have been documented: permanent submersion in water (eggs hatch in approximately 14–21 days depending on temperature) or dry incubation in moist peat/coir for approximately 3–4 weeks before wetting to trigger hatching. The dry-incubation method allows eggs to be shipped between keepers. Eggs are reported to remain viable for 30–60 days during dry incubation. Fry are relatively large at hatching compared to many nano species and have been reported to accept micro-worms, baby brine shrimp nauplii, and commercial micro-foods from first feeding. Separating adults from spawning substrate or raising fry in a dedicated container is generally recommended as adults will consume eggs and fry.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Used as primary after Aquarium Co-Op returned 404 for a dedicated gardneri page. SeriouslyFish is the first secondary for fish-freshwater per source-urls.json; confirmed scientific name, taxonomy, native range (Nigeria + Cameroon, Cross River/Benue systems), water parameters (20-26°C, pH 6.0-7.5, 18-179 ppm hardness), adult size (50-55mm SL), diet, subspecies, egg incubation timing, and conspecific aggression notes.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name, taxonomy (Nothobranchiidae, Cyprinodontiformes), native range (Cross River / Beno, Nigeria), adult size (6.5 cm TL), water parameters (22-25°C, pH 6.0-7.2, hardness 5-8 dH). Confirmed minimum tank length 60 cm. Size and temperature values averaged with SeriouslyFish to produce composite ranges.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Partial species coverage within a top-5 killifish article (no dedicated gardneri page found). Confirmed adult size (~6 cm / 2.5 in), temperature range broadly 19-26°C, escape risk (tight lid required), breeding ease, and male feistiness / compatibility notes. Used as third source to triangulate size and temperature.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scatterer using spawning mops or fine plants; eggs viable for 30-60 days dry. See breedingNotes for full details.
